title: Chapter 2: Quality Assurance And Testing With Chai - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of Chai assertions, testing styles, functional testing with Chai-HTTP, and headless browser testing

questions.question: What are the three assertion styles provided by Chai?
questions.explanation: Chai provides three assertion styles: assert (TDD style, e.g., assert.equal(a, b)), expect (BDD style, e.g., expect(a).to.equal(b)), and should (BDD style, e.g., a.should.equal(b)). All three styles test the same things but with different syntax preferences.

questions.question: Which Chai assertion checks whether a value is truthy?
questions.explanation: assert.isOk(value) tests that value is truthy (any value that coerces to true). This includes non-zero numbers, non-empty strings, objects, arrays, etc. assert.isTrue() is stricter — it checks for exactly the boolean value true, not just truthy values.

questions.question: What is the difference between assert.equal() and assert.strictEqual() in Chai?

options.text: assert.equal() uses == (loose equality), assert.strictEqual() uses === (strict equality)
assert.equal() compares strings, assert.strictEqual() compares numbers
assert.strictEqual() performs deep comparison of objects

questions.explanation: assert.equal() uses loose equality (==) which allows type coercion (e.g., '3' == 3 is true). assert.strictEqual() uses strict equality (===) which requires both value and type to match (e.g., '3' === 3 is false). Using strictEqual is generally recommended to avoid unexpected coercion.

questions.question: Which Chai assertion verifies that an array includes a specific value?
questions.explanation: assert.include(haystack, needle) checks that an array contains the specified value, a string contains a substring, or an object contains a key. For example, assert.include([1, 2, 3], 2) passes. The expect equivalent is expect([1,2,3]).to.include(2).

questions.question: What does assert.deepEqual() check that assert.equal() does not?

options.text: It checks the type of the values
It recursively compares the properties and values of objects and arrays, not just reference equality
It checks if values are defined

questions.explanation: assert.deepEqual() performs a deep recursive comparison of objects and arrays, checking that all properties and nested values match. assert.equal() only checks reference equality for objects, so { a: 1 } == { a: 1 } is false, but assert.deepEqual({ a: 1 }, { a: 1 }) passes.

questions.question: What does Chai-HTTP allow you to do in your tests?

options.text: Monitor HTTP traffic on a network
Make HTTP requests to your Express app and assert on the responses

questions.explanation: Chai-HTTP is a plugin that lets you make HTTP requests (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to your Express app within tests and assert on the response status, headers, and body. For example: chai.request(app).get('/api/users').end((err, res) => { expect(res).to.have.status(200); }).

questions.question: Which Chai assertion checks the type of a value?
questions.explanation: assert.typeOf(value, name) checks that value's type (using typeof) matches the given string. For example, assert.typeOf('hello', 'string') passes. Chai also provides convenience methods like assert.isString(), assert.isNumber(), assert.isArray(), assert.isObject(), etc.

questions.question: In functional testing with Chai-HTTP, how do you check the HTTP status code of a response?
questions.explanation: Chai-HTTP provides the .status() assertion: expect(res).to.have.status(200). This checks the HTTP status code of the response. Chai-HTTP also adds assertions like .header(), .html, .json, .text, and .redirect for comprehensive HTTP response testing.

questions.question: What is headless browser testing?

options.text: Testing a browser extension's header component
Running browser tests in a browser without a visible graphical user interface
Testing only the HTML <head> section of a page
Testing APIs without using a browser at all

questions.explanation: Headless browser testing runs a real browser engine (like Chromium) without displaying a GUI. This allows automated tests to interact with pages — clicking buttons, filling forms, navigating — just like a user would, but programmatically and much faster. Tools like Puppeteer and Playwright provide headless browser automation.

questions.question: Which Chai assertion checks that an object has a specific property?
questions.explanation: assert.property(object, property) checks that an object has the specified property (using the 'in' operator, which checks own and inherited properties). For example, assert.property({ name: 'John' }, 'name') passes. The expect equivalent is expect(obj).to.have.property('name').
